content.text: Agentic AI applications combine language models, tool calls, memory systems, and orchestration logic into workflows where a single bug can cascade through multiple reasoning steps. A four-layer test pyramid — unit, integration, end-to-end scenario, and LLM evaluation — gives engineering teams a principled way to allocate test effort, control feedback latency, and catch failures at the layer where they are cheapest to fix.
What it is

The test pyramid is a heuristic for structuring automated test suites so that the bulk of tests are fast, cheap, and isolated, while a smaller number of expensive, end-to-end tests cover critical user journeys. Mike Cohn popularized the concept for traditional software; adapting it to generative AI systems requires four distinct layers instead of the original three, because language model output adds a probabilistic quality dimension that deterministic unit and integration tests cannot fully capture.

For an agentic AI application the four layers from base to apex are: (1) unit tests covering deterministic helper code, prompt-builder functions, tool schema validators, and parser utilities; (2) integration tests that wire together one LLM call and its adjacent services using real or stub APIs; (3) end-to-end scenario tests that exercise complete agent workflows across multiple turns; and (4) LLM evaluation tests that score output quality, faithfulness, safety, and instruction-following against labeled or reference datasets.
Why it matters

Without a structured pyramid, teams commonly fall into one of two anti-patterns. The first is an ice-cream cone: a majority of slow, brittle end-to-end tests that take an hour to run, drown CI pipelines in flaky failures, and give developers no signal about which component failed. The second is a unit-test desert: fast tests that verify individual helpers but miss LLM hallucination, tool-call formatting errors, and multi-step reasoning failures entirely.

Agentic systems amplify both failure modes. A single incorrectly structured tool-call JSON can silently abort a ten-step agent loop. A prompt that drifts by twenty tokens can shift an LLM's tool-selection behaviour. Catching these failures at the cheapest possible layer — a unit test for the schema builder, an integration test for the tool invocation, a scenario test for the multi-step flow — is the difference between a two-second fix and a three-hour debugging session in production traces.
Benefits
Fast feedback: unit tests run in milliseconds; developers get failures before committing.
Cost control: LLM API calls are metered; pushing checks to unit tests dramatically reduces token spend in CI.
Precise failure localization: a failure at layer N tells you the problem lives in components tested at layer N, not anywhere else.
Confidence ceiling: each layer adds a distinct category of confidence — correctness, integration fidelity, user-journey validity, and output quality.
Scalable parallel execution: lower layers fan out across many CPU cores with no external dependencies; upper layers can be parallelized across multiple API keys or model endpoints.
Regression protection at the right granularity: prompt regression caught at the eval layer avoids shipping silent quality degradation to production users.
Trade-offs
Building four distinct test harnesses requires more initial investment than a single test type.
LLM eval tests introduce non-determinism; flakiness management (retry budgets, score thresholds) adds operational complexity.
Integration tests still incur real API latency (1–10 seconds per test); a large suite can slow CI to 15–30 minutes.
The pyramid shape is a guideline, not a law: some agentic systems have thin unit test surfaces because most logic lives in prompts, forcing more weight on integration and eval layers.
Eval datasets must be maintained and versioned; stale reference data produces misleading pass/fail results.
How it works
Each layer is defined by three axes: the scope of the system under test (SUT), the degree of isolation from external services, and the type of assertion made.
Layer 1: Unit tests

The SUT is a single function or class with no LLM calls. Assertions are deterministic equality or schema checks. Coverage targets: prompt-building functions produce the correct string template given inputs; tool definitions parse to valid JSON Schema; response parsers extract structured data without errors; retry and backoff utilities behave correctly under mock HTTP errors.
Layer 2: Integration tests

The SUT spans one LLM call and its immediate collaborators: the prompt builder, the API client, tool execution, and result parsing. LLM responses are either real (using a low-cost model or a cached replay) or stubbed via an HTTP intercept library. Assertions check structural correctness — does the model return a valid tool_call object, does the tool executor run and return the expected shape, does the parser produce a typed result — not semantic quality.
Layer 3: E2E scenario tests

The SUT is a complete agent workflow from user input to final response, running against a real or staging LLM endpoint. Test cases represent named scenarios: a happy-path research task, a clarification loop, an error-recovery branch. Assertions verify goal completion (did the agent retrieve the right document, produce a response with required fields), turn count bounds, and absence of tool errors. These tests are slower (10–120 seconds) and fewer in number.
Layer 4: LLM evaluation tests

The SUT is the quality of model output against a reference dataset. An LLM judge or scoring function evaluates dimensions such as faithfulness to retrieved context, instruction-following rate, refusal accuracy on adversarial inputs, and toxicity scores. Results are tracked as metrics over time, not binary pass/fail per run. Regressions trigger CI failures when a score drops below a configured threshold.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Four-layer test pyramid for agentic AI applications showing unit, integration, E2E, and LLM eval layers

content.text: The diagram above shows the relative volume of tests at each layer and the tradeoff axes. The base (unit) is widest — hundreds to thousands of tests running in milliseconds. Integration tests form the second band — tens to low hundreds of tests completing in seconds. E2E scenario tests form the third band — ten to fifty scenarios completing in minutes. LLM eval runs sit at the apex — a scored batch job that may run nightly or on model/prompt changes.
When to use it

Apply the full four-layer pyramid when the application has significant deterministic logic around the LLM (prompt builders, tool schemas, parsers, orchestration state machines) and when output quality is a measurable product requirement. A simple single-turn chatbot with no tools and no structured output may not justify a dedicated eval layer; a multi-agent research pipeline with structured output, file writes, and external API calls justifies all four layers.
Use it when CI must stay under 20 minutes: push the bulk of assertions to unit and integration layers.
Use it when LLM costs are a concern: cached or mocked integration tests avoid per-run token charges.
Skip the eval layer when output quality is inherently subjective and no reference dataset exists — invest first in integration tests.
Skip the E2E layer for purely programmatic agents where integration tests already cover all state transitions.
How to implement

A practical implementation follows five steps: scaffold the test directory structure, write unit tests for all pure functions, configure integration test fixtures with HTTP replay or model stubs, author E2E scenario test cases, and set up the eval harness with a scoring function and threshold configuration.

# Directory structure for a Python agentic application
tests/
  unit/
    test_prompt_builders.py
    test_tool_schemas.py
    test_parsers.py
    test_retry_utils.py
  integration/
    conftest.py          # HTTP replay fixtures
    test_llm_calls.py
    test_tool_execution.py
    test_memory_retrieval.py
  e2e/
    scenarios/
      test_research_workflow.py
      test_clarification_loop.py
      test_error_recovery.py
    conftest.py          # Staging endpoint config
  eval/
    datasets/
      faithfulness_v1.jsonl
      instruction_follow_v1.jsonl
    test_eval_suite.py
    thresholds.yaml

# tests/unit/test_tool_schemas.py
import pytest
import json
import jsonschema
from myagent.tools import build_search_tool_def, build_write_tool_def

def test_search_tool_def_is_valid_json_schema():
    tool_def = build_search_tool_def()
    assert tool_def["type"] == "function"
    schema = tool_def["function"]["parameters"]
    # jsonschema.validate raises SchemaError if the schema itself is invalid
    jsonschema.Draft7Validator.check_schema(schema)

def test_search_tool_requires_query_parameter():
    tool_def = build_search_tool_def()
    params = tool_def["function"]["parameters"]
    assert "query" in params["properties"]
    assert "query" in params.get("required", [])

def test_write_tool_def_filename_pattern():
    tool_def = build_write_tool_def()
    params = tool_def["function"]["parameters"]
    filename_schema = params["properties"]["filename"]
    assert "pattern" in filename_schema, "filename must restrict path traversal via pattern"

def test_tool_name_no_spaces():
    for tool_def in [build_search_tool_def(), build_write_tool_def()]:
        name = tool_def["function"]["name"]
        assert " " not in name, f"Tool name '{name}' must not contain spaces"
Examples
Integration test with HTTP replay
E2E scenario test
Real-world example

A financial-research agentic system at a mid-size asset manager runs 1,200 unit tests (sub-second), 80 integration tests using VCR cassette replay (45 seconds), 18 E2E scenario tests against a staging LLM endpoint (8 minutes), and a nightly eval suite scoring 400 reference queries across four quality dimensions (20 minutes). The total CI wall time for each pull request is 10 minutes because the eval suite runs asynchronously on a separate schedule.

The team discovered three distinct failure categories that only manifested at specific pyramid layers: prompt-template whitespace bugs caught by unit tests within seconds of introduction; tool argument type coercion errors caught by integration tests before any agent logic ran; and a silent hallucination regression on numerical queries caught by the eval suite after a model upgrade — none of which were visible in E2E scenario assertions because the answer format was still structurally valid.
Common mistakes
Writing only E2E tests: slow suite, no failure localization, expensive token spend per CI run.
Mocking the LLM entirely in integration tests: misses tool-call format changes when the underlying model changes.
Using identical prompts in unit tests and production: unit tests that hardcode prompt strings diverge from the real prompt builder under refactoring.
Not versioning eval datasets: a dataset that drifts alongside the model makes it impossible to detect regressions.
Setting eval score thresholds too tight: a 0.1-point fluctuation in LLM judge scores triggers false positives and trains the team to ignore CI failures.
Skipping the integration layer and going straight from unit to E2E: tool-schema errors and API contract bugs are invisible at the unit layer and expensive to debug in E2E runs.
Best practices
Run layers in order during CI: unit → integration → E2E → eval, with each gate blocking the next to fail fast.
Keep unit test coverage above 80% for all deterministic code paths.
Use cassette-based HTTP replay (vcrpy, respx, nock) for integration tests to make them deterministic and free.
Tag E2E tests with markers and run them only on PR merges to main, not on every commit.
Track eval metrics as time-series in a dashboard; treat threshold crossings as incidents, not test failures to be rerun.
Seed the integration test fixture with at least one tool-call response and one non-tool-call response to cover both branches.
Use a lightweight model (GPT-4o-mini, Claude Haiku) for integration test real-API runs to contain cost.
Performance & scalability

The pyramid shape is itself a performance architecture. At scale, the cost of running the full suite grows with the number of integration and E2E tests, not unit tests. A practical capacity model for a team of eight engineers running 50 PRs per day:
1,000 unit tests at 2 ms each = 2 seconds total; trivially parallelizable.
100 integration tests at 1.5 seconds each = 150 seconds with 10x parallelism = 15 seconds wall time.
20 E2E tests at 30 seconds each = 600 seconds with 5x parallelism = 2 minutes wall time.
Eval suite at 20 minutes runs nightly or on model/prompt changes only — not per PR.

Integration tests should never call production LLM endpoints in CI. Use replay cassettes for standard tests and a dedicated low-cost test API key for the subset that must run live. Rate-limit integration test parallelism to avoid hitting API concurrency caps.
Tooling & ecosystem
pytest
+ pytest-asyncio: standard Python test runner with async support for LLM clients.
vcrpy / respx
: HTTP cassette libraries for replaying LLM API responses in integration tests.
deepeval
: open-source LLM evaluation framework with built-in metrics (faithfulness, answer relevancy, hallucination).
langsmith
: LangChain's tracing and evaluation platform; captures agent traces and runs evals over them.
pytest-timeout
: enforces time limits on E2E tests to prevent hanging agents from blocking CI.
hypothesis
: property-based testing library useful for fuzzing prompt builder inputs and parser edge cases.
ragas
: RAG-specific evaluation metrics (context recall, context precision) useful for retrieval-augmented agents.

Security & reliability

The test pyramid itself is a reliability mechanism: each layer enforces a contractual boundary that prevents regressions from reaching higher layers and production. From a security perspective, integration tests should cover tool authorization checks — does the agent correctly refuse to execute a write_file tool when the user session lacks the required permission. E2E tests should include adversarial scenarios: a prompt injection attempt, a malformed tool response, and a context window overflow scenario.

Cassette files for integration tests must not contain real API keys or user data. Scrub credentials and PII from cassette fixtures before committing them to the repository. Eval datasets containing sensitive reference content should be stored in an access-controlled artifact store, not in the application repository.
Key takeaways
The four-layer pyramid — unit, integration, E2E, LLM eval — adapts the classic test pyramid to handle both deterministic logic and probabilistic output quality.
Each layer has a distinct assertion type: schema correctness (unit), structural API correctness (integration), goal completion (E2E), and semantic quality (eval).
Cost and speed are controlled by pushing the most assertions to the cheapest layer; only a small fraction of tests should touch a real LLM endpoint in CI.
Cassette-based HTTP replay makes integration tests deterministic and free without losing coverage of the LLM API contract.
The eval layer is the only mechanism that catches prompt-level quality regressions; it should run on every model or prompt change, even if not on every commit.
Skipping the integration layer is the most common structural mistake — it leaves tool-schema bugs and API contract issues invisible until E2E tests catch them expensively.
Tag tests by layer and gate CI stages on each layer in sequence for the fastest possible feedback loop.
